1: Samson's story begins with an unauthorized desire that YHWH sovereignly uses: 'his father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH' (v4). This is the pattern of divine sovereignty working through human failure — what Joseph names in Genesis 50:20: 'You intended evil against me; God intended it for good.' In Christ, the ultimate example of this principle reaches its apex: those who crucified him 'acted in ignorance' (Acts 3:17), yet 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' (Acts 2:23). The cross is simultaneously human evil and divine good — the same God who worked through Samson's sinful desire works through the sinful desire of those who condemned his Son.
4: 'It was from YHWH, for he was seeking an occasion against the Philistines.' Divine sovereignty over human sin — the most theologically dense verse in the Samson narrative. YHWH does not author Samson's sinful desire but works through it to create the confrontation his deliverance purpose requires. This is the grammar of providence at the cross: 'for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel, to do whatever your hand and your plan had predestined to take place' (Acts 4:27-28). The 'occasion' YHWH seeks through Samson's marriage is a type of the 'occasion' he sought through the betrayal of Judas, the jealousy of the priests, and the cowardice of Pilate.
6: 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him' — the first of three Spirit-empowerments in the Samson narrative (Judges 14:6; Judges 14:19; Judges 15:14). Each prepares for a specific act of deliverance. The permanent, immeasurable Spirit-anointing of Christ (John 3:34 — 'for he gives the Spirit without measure') is the fulfillment: not crisis-response empowerment but abiding consecration. The lion-tearing — bare-handed, no weapon — is Samson at his most messianic: pure divine strength operating through human flesh. Paul: 'not I, but the grace of God that is with me' (1 Corinthians 15:10). The Spirit-empowered defeat of a predatory beast anticipates Christ's defeat of the lion-like adversary: 'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (1 Peter 5:8) — Christ 'destroyed the one who has the power of death' (Hebrews 2:14).
14: 'Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet.' The Samson riddle is perhaps the most compressed type of the resurrection in the OT: death (the lion, the eater, the strong) produces life (honey, food, sweetness). Paul's resurrection hymn draws from the same imagery: 'Death is swallowed up in victory. O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?' (1 Corinthians 15:54-55 — citing Isaiah 25:8 and Hosea 13:14). The strength of death has been inverted into the sweetness of resurrection life. Christ's death — the ultimate 'eater' that consumed the sins of the world — becomes the source of the sweetest good: eternal life. 'Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24).
19: 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon and struck thirty men of them.' The second Spirit-empowerment in a context that looks like personal vengeance rather than covenant deliverance. Yet even here YHWH is working — these are the first Philistines struck in the campaign that will culminate in Judges 16. Christ's statement about his own mission maps this pattern: 'I did not come to bring peace, but a sword' (Matthew 10:34) — what appears as personal confrontation (the temple cleansing, the clash with Pharisees) is the divine warrior's campaign against the powers that enslave humanity. The Spirit-rushed judge who strikes the enemy in the context of personal crisis is a rough type of the Spirit-anointed Messiah whose personal sufferings become the mechanism of universal deliverance.
2: Samson reports to his father and mother: 'I saw one of the daughters of the Philistines at Timnah; now get her for me as my wife.' The direct imperative 'get her for me' bypasses the usual paternal mediation of betrothal, with Samson acting as if his desire is self-authorizing. The Christological inversion: where Samson imposes his will on his father for a bride from the wrong people, the Son of God submits his will to the Father (John 6:38) to obtain a Bride from every people — the Church gathered from the nations. Samson's bridal quest, driven by personal desire, is the shadow; Christ's acquisition of his Bride, driven by the Father's love, is the fulfillment (Ephesians 5:25-27).
3: The parents' objection — 'Is there not a woman among the daughters of your relatives, or among all our people, that you must go to take a wife from the uncircumcised Philistines?' — raises the covenant barrier: Samson is reaching across the separation between Israel and the nations. The uncircumcised (ʾărēlîm) were excluded from covenant standing (Genesis 17:14; Exodus 12:48). But v4 establishes that YHWH is working through Samson's transgression of boundaries. The type extends to Christ who crosses the ultimate covenant barrier — between God and humanity — to take a Bride from those who were 'alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise' (Ephesians 2:12). The Bride is, from every cultural vantage point, the 'wrong' choice — uncircumcised, Gentile, once without God.
5: As Samson goes down to Timnah, a young lion comes roaring toward him — and the Spirit of YHWH rushes upon him, enabling him to tear the lion apart with his bare hands. The lion-kill is the first of three Spirit-empowering events in Samson's narrative (Judges 14:6, Judges 14:19, Judges 15:14). The Spirit as the enabler of lion-mastery connects to the promise of Psalms 91:13 ('you will tread on the lion') and to the messianic figure of Genesis 49:9 (Judah as a lion). The Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) slays the devouring lion: Christ, empowered by the Spirit in his earthly ministry (Luke 4:18), overcomes the 'roaring lion' that seeks to devour (1 Peter 5:8). The young lion (kᵉpîr) that threatens Samson is the same word used in Psalms 34:10 for the strong who go hungry; Samson's defeat of it anticipates the triumph over every power that threatens the anointed one.
7: Samson goes down and speaks to the woman and she is pleasing in Samson's eyes — the language of mutual recognition and covenant pledge. The exchange between Samson and the Timnite woman initiates the betrothal that YHWH will use to provoke conflict with the Philistines. The typological frame: the engagement of the Son of God with his Bride is also an act that provokes conflict with the ruling powers of the age. Christ's covenant with his Church — sealed in his blood (Matthew 26:28) — brings him into direct confrontation with the 'rulers of this age' who crucify him (1 Corinthians 2:8), just as Samson's covenant with the Philistine woman generates the conflict that will ultimately destroy Philistine power.
8: When Samson returns to take the woman, he turns aside to see the lion's carcass — and behold, a swarm of bees with honey. The honey in the dead lion is the narrative's central image and the basis for the riddle. The transformation of death into sweetness — the place of destruction becoming a source of nourishment — is the precise grammar of the atonement. Paul uses this logic in 1 Corinthians 15:55-56 ('O death, where is your sting?') and in Galatians 3:13-14 (the curse of the cross becomes the blessing of the Spirit). Out of the dead lion — the place of Samson's act of violent killing — comes honey. Out of the dead Christ — the site of God's act of sacrificial giving — comes 'the fruit of the Spirit' (Galatians 5:22): sweetness produced in the death of the strong.
9: Samson scoops the honey into his hands and eats as he walks, giving some to his father and mother — but he does not tell them it came from the lion's carcass (which would have made them ritually unclean, Leviticus 11:39). The sharing of honey from an unclean source, received without the receiver knowing its origin, is a complex typological image: the blessing that comes through Christ's death (the broken, 'unclean' body) is distributed to those who receive it without fully understanding its source. The disciples receive the bread and cup at the Last Supper without fully comprehending that they are receiving the fruit of Christ's death. The honey-bread shared from the death of the strong is the Eucharistic pattern in nascent form.
10: His father goes down to the woman and Samson makes a feast there — the wedding banquet. The seven-day wedding feast (mišteh) in an enemy territory is a type of the eschatological wedding banquet of Revelation 19:7-9 hosted by the greater Son of David in a world that has not yet been fully subdued. The feast within enemy territory — celebrated while the adversary watches — prefigures the Church's Eucharistic feast celebrated during the age when the enemy has not yet been fully destroyed but has already been decisively defeated at the cross. The marriage supper is real even though the battle continues.
11: When the Philistines see him, they bring thirty companions to be with him. The thirty companions assigned to watch Samson — or as his groomsmen — are the narrative setup for the riddle-wager. The number thirty is typologically significant: thirty pieces of silver is the price Judas receives for betraying Jesus (Matthew 26:15; Zechariah 11:12-13). The thirty companions of Samson who become the instruments of his betrayal through his wife parallel Judas and the thirty silver pieces — in both cases, an intimate mediates betrayal, money is the mechanism, and the betrayal results in the anointed one's captivity and humiliation.
12: Samson offers the riddle for the seven-day feast with clothing as the prize: 'if you can tell me within the seven days of the feast what it is, I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes.' The seven days of the riddle-contest maps onto the seven days of creation and rest, suggesting that Samson's contest is a microcosm of the covenant order. The prize of garments (linen and festival clothing) anticipates the NT's imagery of the Bride clothed in fine linen (Revelation 19:8: 'the fine linen is the righteous deeds of the saints') — the garments that the Bridegroom provides to those who solve the mystery of his death and resurrection.
13: The Philistines accept the wager: if Samson tells them the answer, they give him the same quantity of clothing. The reciprocal wager establishes a covenant-contest over the mystery of Samson's strength. The pattern of a divine mystery embedded in apparent defeat — which the enemy cannot solve without the help of the anointed one's intimate — extends into the NT's theology of the 'mystery hidden for ages' (Colossians 1:26; 1 Corinthians 2:7). The rulers of this age could not solve the riddle of the cross and so 'crucified the Lord of glory' (1 Corinthians 2:8) — they could not discern that the death of the strong would produce the sweetness of resurrection.
15: On the fourth day the Philistine companions threaten Samson's wife: 'Entice your husband to tell us the riddle, or we will burn you and your father's house with fire.' The threat of fire against the woman and her house is the mechanism that will eventually destroy both (Judges 15:6); the enemies' coercion of Samson's intimate partner is the first instance of the betrayal pattern that will reach its fullest expression in Judges 16. The threatened fire prefigures the eschatological fire of judgment (2 Thessalonians 1:7-8) — the Philistines deploy it as a weapon of coercion, but YHWH uses fire as the instrument of ultimate judgment on the same enemies (Judges 15:5).
16: His wife weeps before him and says, 'You only hate me; you do not love me. You have told the riddle to the sons of my people and have not told me.' The accusation 'you hate me' deployed as emotional leverage inverts the truth: Samson's refusal to tell the riddle is not hatred but the protection of the mystery's integrity. The Christological inversion: Christ's apparent silence before Pilate (Matthew 27:12-14) and his refusal to perform signs on demand (Luke 23:8-9) are also read by the hostile parties as indifference or weakness, but they are the sovereign protection of the divine mystery from those who would use it destructively. The anointed one's silence is not absence but wisdom.
17: She weeps the remaining seven days of the feast; on the seventh day he tells her because she pressed him hard. The breaking of Samson's resistance on the seventh day anticipates the pattern of Judges 16 (Delilah presses him until his soul is vexed to death, v16); both collapses occur through sustained intimate pressure, and both produce catastrophic consequences. The seventh day is the day of completion and rest — Samson's revelation of the mystery on the Sabbath-day echoes the pattern of the cross on Friday followed by revelation on Sunday: the mystery of the anointed one is revealed at the moment of apparent defeat.
18: 'What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' the Philistines answer. Samson responds: 'If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found out my riddle.' The image of the heifer plowed by another is a covenant-violation idiom: the woman betrothed to one man used by another. Samson's riddled response — the heifer metaphor — encodes both the betrayal of intimacy and the deeper mystery: the answer to his riddle ('from the eater comes food, from the strong comes sweetness') is YHWH's way of working through apparent defeat. The Philistines have the answer but not the understanding. Knowing the riddle's words is not the same as understanding its theology, as Paul argues in 1 Corinthians 1:18-25: the message of the cross is 'foolishness' to those who solve it by external information rather than Spirit-given revelation.
20: Samson's wife is given to his companion who had been his best man. The dissolution of Samson's first marriage — the bride given to the companion rather than to the groom — is the reversal of the covenant order. In the eschatological register, the Bride belongs to Christ the Bridegroom (John 3:29: 'the friend of the bridegroom, who stands and hears him, rejoices greatly at the bridegroom's voice'); the giving of the bride to the companion is the violation that the narrative builds toward correcting. Samson's first marriage is dissolved by betrayal; the covenant marriage of Christ and his Church is secured by his blood, and no betrayal can dissolve it (John 10:28-29; Romans 8:35-39).
